title-group.article-title: Effectiveness and safety of darunavir and lopinavir for treating children and adolescents living with HIV: a systematic review

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Darunavir and lopinavir solid formulations are recommended protease inhibitors for treating HIV. An evidence review of their effectiveness/efficacy and safety in young populations was needed to inform the 2025 World Health Organization guidelines.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We conducted a systematic review of publications reporting efficacy/effectiveness and/or safety of darunavir and/or lopinavir solid formulations in children and/or adolescents with HIV aged 0–19 years, with at least 6 months follow-up. Sources included MEDLINE, Embase, Cochrane Library, recent HIV conferences and trial registries. Data were synthesized narratively.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: We screened 4,196 abstracts and 421 papers. Thirteen studies (including two randomized controlled trials (RCTs)) on darunavir were identified in 1,895 children/adolescents (2% treatment-naïve). Among treatment-experienced participants on ritonavir-boosted darunavir, 86% (198/229) and 95% (143/150) had viral suppression (< 50 or < 400 copies/mL) at 48 weeks in the RCTs and 29% (4/14)-100% (12/12) across seven other studies (N = 736), respectively. Participants in the four studies (N = 184) with < 70% suppression were heavily treatment-experienced and unsuppressed at darunavir start. Grade 3/4 adverse events (clinical/laboratory) occurred in 0%-30% of participants on ritonavir-boosted darunavir (seven studies, N = 515; 8–9% in two large RCTs), and adverse events leading to discontinuation or treatment modification in 0%-10% (10 studies, N = 974; 2–3% in two RCTs). Three grade 3/4 adverse events in one study were drug related. Twenty-nine studies (including four randomized trials) on ritonavir-boosted lopinavir solid formulations were identified in 3,605 children/adolescents (17% treatment-naïve). Among treatment-experienced children/adolescents at 48–52 weeks, viral suppression was 80% (179/223) in one RCT and 53% (448/852)-100% (12/12) across five other studies (N = 1305). Across all populations, grade 3/4 adverse events were reported in 8%-60% of participants (four studies, N = 1405; 8–13% in two large RCTs), and events leading to discontinuation or treatment modification in 0%-9% (seven studies, N = 905; 2–3% in two RCTs). Five participants experienced drug-related grade 3/4 adverse events (five studies, N = 1107). Lipid levels were significantly greater with lopinavir versus other regimens (five studies).

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Darunavir was safe in treatment-experienced children and adolescents, and viral suppression was high in RCTs; data were limited in treatment-naïve populations. Viral suppression with lopinavir solid formulations was variable. Lopinavir was generally well-tolerated but compared unfavourably with other drugs in terms of lipid outcomes.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12879-025-12511-z.

kwd-group.title: Keywords
kwd-group.kwd: Drug-related side effects and adverse reactions

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: An estimated 2.58 million children and adolescents aged 0–19 years were living with HIV in 2022 [1]. The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends initiating therapy for all children living with HIV regardless of HIV disease stage or CD4 count [2]. Darunavir (DRV) and lopinavir (LPV) are protease inhibitors (PIs) that are usually administered with low-dose ritonavir, another PI. Ritonavir-boosted DRV (DRV/r) was approved for use in children aged ≥ 3 years in 2011 [3, 4] and is recommended in the 2021 WHO treatment guidelines as an alternative option for second-line therapy in children and adolescents if dolutegravir-based regimens have failed and as third-line therapy [2]. DRV and ritonavir are currently available as separate tablets or oral solutions for children. A solid paediatric fixed-dose combination of DRV/r (120/20 mg) is currently under investigation [5]. An alternative DRV booster, cobicistat, is also being investigated in populations ≥ 3 years old [6].

Ritonavir-boosted LPV (LPV/r) is recommended as an alternative first-line regimen for children if dolutegravir is unsuitable or inaccessible and as a preferred second-line regimen where dolutegravir-based regimens have failed [2]. Since 2008, it has been licenced in neonates > 42 weeks gestation and at least 14 days old, with paediatric formulations including an oral solution, pellets, granules and tablets. WHO no longer recommends LPV/r oral solution as the preferred formulation for neonates, infants and young children because of poor palatability, the need for refrigeration, and toxicity potential in neonates, possibly due to alcohol and propylene glycol content [7]. Alternative preferred LPV/r formulations for younger children unable to swallow tablets include heat-stable taste-masked oral pellets and dispersible granules, although supply issues have limited the use of these alternatives [8]. Given the challenges of using these formulations, WHO recommends LPV/r tablets for children weighing ≥ 10 kg who can swallow tablets.

The evidence base for ritonavir- or cobicistat-boosted DRV and LPV/r solid formulations in young populations has not been previously summarized. In 2025, WHO was considering whether DRV/r should be recommended as the preferred rather than alternative second-line option for treatment across age groups. We therefore undertook a systematic review on the effectiveness/efficacy and safety of these two PIs in infants, children and adolescents living with HIV to support the development of the 2025 WHO updated recommendations on HIV clinical management.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The systematic review followed the Centre for Reviews and Disseminations guidelines and was reported according to PRISMA guidelines [9, 10].

sec.title: Protocol
sec.p: A protocol covering five drugs—DRV, LPV, dolutegravir, raltegravir, and tenofovir alafenamide—was published in advance on PROSPERO (CRD42020204432) [11]. Results pertaining to DRV and LPV are presented here, with other results reported elsewhere [12, 13]. Amendments to the protocol after publication included adding eligibility criteria (discussed below) to facilitate screening of LPV studies; adding risk of bias tools for single-arm and cluster-randomized trials (described below); and adding end points related to changes in lipid values from baseline.

sec.title: Search strategies, data sources, and eligibility criteria
sec.p: MEDLINE, Embase (both via Ovid SP), and the Cochrane Library were searched with terms for infants, children and adolescents; treatments of interest; and study design filters. We limited searches to papers published from 01/01/2009, after the approval of DRV for paediatric use and coinciding with WHO recommendations to phase out didanosine and stavudine in 2009–2010, to 20/09/2023. Grey literature searches included reference lists of included studies, relevant guidelines, recent key conferences, and clinical trial registries (clinicaltrials.gov, WHO and European trial platforms). Details of the data sources and search strategies are available in Additional File 1 (Sections S1-S3).

Eligibility criteria for the review are described in Table 1. Briefly, observational and interventional studies were included if they assessed effectiveness or efficacy outcomes (referred to as effectiveness in this article) and/or safety outcomes in populations aged 0–19 years, living with HIV, regardless of previous treatment experience, and receiving DRV or LPV solid formulations for ≥ 6 months. Study authors were contacted for clarification when needed.

caption.p: Eligibility criteria for the selection of included studies

tr.td: Children and adolescents (aged 0–19 years†) living with HIV regardless of previous treatment experience

Ritonavir- or cobicistat-boosted darunavir or ritonavir-boosted lopinavir (LPV/r; solid formulation only) given in combination with any other antiretroviral therapies recommended for use in a paediatric or adolescent population
Dolutegravir-, raltegravir-, or tenofovir alafenamide-based regimens or no comparator (i.e., single arm trials were included)‡

td.p: (all measured through 6 months follow-up or more)
• Treatment discontinuation or change and reasons
• Grade 3/4 adverse events (including psychiatric events) and their association with antiretroviral therapy, including hospitalization
• Lipid levels (cholesterol and triglycerides; change from baseline)

tr.td: Observational studies or clinical trials (case studies were excluded)
Published between 1st January 2009 and date of searches ¶

td.bold: Additional criteria for studies assessing LPV/r to account for studies with mixed populations

tr.td: • If the median age of the population was ≤ 5 years and if the publication did not specify the formulation of LPV/r, the formulation was assumed to be liquid and the study was excluded.
• If > 20% of the population of the study was receiving didanosine or stavudine (no longer recommended) the study was excluded.

• If the NRTI backbone was not reported the study was excluded if the number of participants was < 100. For studies with n ≥ 100 participants, study authors were contacted for additional information; if no further information was provided, the study was excluded.

table-wrap-foot.p: † Studies reporting on an adult population that included participants aged ≥ 18 years only were excluded, as were studies assessing the effect of exposure to antiretroviral drugs during pregnancy or postnatally. ‡ For randomized controlled trials only, data from comparator arms were extracted regardless of the comparator treatment. § These outcomes were reported in a small number of studies and are summarized in Additional File 1 (Section S6). ¶ Searches were initially run in August 2020, and update searches were run in March 2021 and September 2023

sec.p: During screening, we identified several studies assessing LPV that did not report the formulation (including mixed populations receiving solid and liquid formulations) or included participants receiving the nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) didanosine or stavudine, which are no longer recommended. To manage this, we excluded studies if the LPV formulation was not specified and the median age was ≤ 5 years; if > 20% of the population received didanosine or stavudine; or if the NRTI backbone was not reported and the study included < 100 participants; for studies with ≥ 100 participants, the authors were contacted for clarification.

sec.title: Article screening and data extraction
sec.p: Abstracts and full papers were screened in duplicate (by JOR, CT and EM). Records were managed using Endnote X9 and Microsoft Excel, and data extraction and risk of bias assessment were undertaken in a standard Excel form by one reviewer and checked for accuracy by a second reviewer. Data reported only in figures were extracted using the online tool “Web Plot Digitizer” [14]. Disagreements at all stages were resolved through discussion or with assistance from a third reviewer.

Data on study, participant and treatment characteristics and effectiveness and safety outcomes were extracted. The primary and secondary outcomes are shown in Table 1, and the complete list of prespecified outcomes is provided in Additional File 1 (Section S6). For categorical endpoints, we extracted the number and proportion of participants experiencing an outcome. For continuous outcomes, we extracted the mean, median, standard deviation (SD), interquartile range (IQR), range, change from baseline and 95% confidence interval (95% CI).

During the initial screening process, we identified studies reporting endpoints that were not in our protocol but were related to prespecified outcomes (e.g., other bone, renal, and lipid parameters). A summary of these outcomes is provided in Additional File 1 (Section S9, ‘Exploratory analyses’) but should not be considered a comprehensive review of these outcomes.

We selected three risk of bias tools appropriate for the designs of the included studies: the Cochrane risk of bias tool (Version 2) for randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and cluster RCTs, the National Institute of Health quality assessment tool for single-arm trials, and the CLARITY tool for observational studies [15–18]. Table 2 lists the questions used for each tool.

caption.p: List of domains or questions used to assess the risk of bias for randomized controlled trials, single-arm trials, and observational studies

tr.td: Domain 1: Risk of bias from the randomization process
Domain 2: Risk of bias due to deviations from the intended interventions
Domain 4: Risk of bias in measurement of the outcome
Domain 5: Risk of bias in selection of the reported result
Qu 1. Was the study question or objective clearly stated?
Qu 2. Were eligibility/selection criteria for the study population prespecified and clearly described?
Qu 3. Were the participants in the study representative of those who would be eligible for the intervention in the general or clinical population of interest?
Qu 4. Were all eligible participants that met the prespecified entry criteria enrolled?
Qu 5. Was the sample size sufficiently large to provide confidence in the findings?
Qu 6. Was the intervention clearly described and delivered consistently across the study population?
Qu 7. Were the outcome measures prespecified, clearly defined, valid, reliable, and assessed consistently across all study participants?
Qu 8. Were the people assessing the outcomes blinded to the participants’ interventions?
Qu 9. Was the loss to follow-up after baseline 20% or less? Were those lost to follow-up accounted for in the analysis?
Qu 10. Did the statistical methods examine changes in outcome measures from before to after the intervention? Were statistical tests done that provided p values for the pre-to-post changes?
Qu 11. Were outcome measures of interest taken at baseline and multiple times after the intervention?
Qu 12. If the intervention was conducted at a group level did the statistical analysis take into account the use of individual-level data to determine effects at the group level?
Qu 1. Was the study population selected in an appropriate way?
Qu 2. Can we be confident in the assessment of exposure?
Qu 3. Can we be confident that the outcome of interest was not present at start of study for (a) effectiveness and, (b) safety?

Qu 4. For comparative studies: did the study match exposed and unexposed for all variables that are associated with the outcome of interest or did the statistical analysis adjust for these prognostic variables?
Qu 5. Can we be confident in the assessment of the presence or absence of prognostic factors?
Qu 6. Can we be confident in the assessment of outcome for (a) effectiveness and, (b) safety?
Qu 7. Was the follow up of cohorts adequate? (both duration and completeness of follow-up i.e. are all subjects accounted for? )
Qu 8. For comparative studies: were co-interventions similar between groups?

table-wrap-foot.p: Questions taken from † the Cochrane Risk of Bias Tool (Version 2) for randomized controlled trials and the adapted tool for cluster randomized trials; ‡ the National Institutes of Health quality assessment tool for nonrandomized interventional studies; § the CLARITY tool for cohort studies

sec.title: Evidence synthesis
sec.p: Data were synthesized narratively and are summarized in tables and figures. Viral load scatterplots were prepared using STATA 18. All other figures were created in Microsoft Excel.

sec.title: Definitions
sec.p: Infants were defined as < 12 months old, children as 1 to < 12 years old, and adolescents as 12 to < 20 years old. Participants were defined as treatment-naïve if they had not previously used antiretroviral therapy (ART) and treatment-experienced if they had previously received ART regardless of the reason for switching regimens. Treatment was defined as ‘mixed experience’ if a study included treatment-naïve and treatment-experienced participants and if the results were not stratified. Studies were defined as including virally suppressed participants at the start of treatment if the study authors defined them as such. At follow-up, the suppression thresholds were < 50, < 400, or < 1000 copies/mL.

Adverse events were reported as grade 3 (severe) or grade 4 (life threatening or disabling) according to standard definitions and as reported in the publications; these could be due to clinical signs or symptoms or laboratory findings [19]. We report the number of participants who experienced at least one grade 3 or 4 adverse event rather than the total number of events. We did not extract grade 1 or 2 events or ‘serious adverse events’ (unless the grade was ≥ 3).

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: Searches were run on 12/08/2020 and updated on 21/03/2021 and 20/09/2023. A PRISMA diagram is presented in Fig. 1, with diagrams for individual searches presented in Additional File 1 (Section S4). Database searching identified 4,196 abstracts, and 421 were shortlisted for full-text review. We included 46 articles identified through database searching and 14 conference abstracts. Through reference searching, we identified two additional papers [20, 21]; one was published before the cut-off date for the review [21] but was an earlier publication of an included study (HIV-NAT 017) [22], and we therefore included it. The results from the CHAPAS-4 RCT were included as two conference presentations, and extracted data points were checked and updated on the basis of results subsequently posted on medRxiv after the search dates of our review [23–25]; however, no additional endpoints were extracted. Of the 62 publications, 54 (reporting on 41 studies) contributed to the primary analysis, and eight (reporting on six studies) contributed to the exploratory analyses of non-prespecified outcomes. Screening of trial registries identified one additional study, UNIVERSAL2, which is evaluating a new formulation of DRV/r for children aged over 3 years (expected completion: June 2026) (Additional File 1, Section S10).

caption.p: PRISMA flow diagram summarizing the inclusion and exclusion of studies at each stage of the review. Note: The search strategies used for the original searches and 2021 update included terms for darunavir, lopinavir, dolutegravir, raltegravir, and tenofovir alafenamide; only studies assessing darunavir and lopinavir are included here. † Of the 235 publications excluded because they assessed an intervention other than darunavir or lopinavir, two articles reported data on tenofovir alafenamide, and 19 reported data on dolutegravir or raltegravir. Abbreviations: DRV, darunavir; LPV, lopinavir

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p: Thirteen studies assessing the effectiveness and/or safety of DRV in 1895 children and adolescents were included (Table 3): two RCTs [23, 26], five single-arm trials [27–31], and six cohort studies [32–38]. One of the single-arm trials, DIANA [30], consisted of long-term follow-up of a subset of participants enrolled in three of the other single-arm trials: DIONE, ARIEL, and DELPHI [27–29]. Nine of the studies were conducted across multiple countries, and four were conducted in a single country. Three studies each included primarily children or adolescents, and seven included both (Table 3). Ten studies included exclusively treatment-experienced populations, and one study included only treatment-naïve participants [27]. All studies followed up participants for ≥ 48 weeks. In eight studies, participants were viraemic at the start of DRV. Cobicistat-boosted DRV (DRV/cobicistat) was used in one study (Mujuru 2023; trial number GS-US-216-0128) [31], and DRV/r was used in the remainder; results are presented separately.

Abbreviations: AE, adverse events; ART, antiretroviral therapy; ATV/r, ritonavir-boosted atazanavir; BID, twice daily; DRV/c, cobicistat-boosted darunavir; DRV/r, ritonavir-boosted darunavir; DTG, dolutegravir; HAZ, height-for-age z score; FU, follow-up; INSTI, integrase strand transfer inhibitor; IQR, interquartile range; LPV/r, ritonavir-boosted lopinavir; LTFU, loss to follow-up; QD, once daily; SoC, standard of care; VL, viral load < 50 copies/mL, < 400 copies/mL, or < 1000 copies/mL, as reported in the publication; WAZ, weight-for-age z score

sec.title: Risk of bias
sec.p: The results from the risk of bias assessment are summarized in Fig. 2 and Additional File 1 (Section S5). Both RCTs and four single-arm trials were classified as having a low risk of bias, whereas the fifth single-arm trial was classified as having an unclear risk. Thuret 2009 was the only cohort study deemed to have a low risk of bias.

caption.p: Summary of the risk of bias assessment categorized by study design and treatment: (a) randomized controlled trials, (b) single-arm trials, and (c) observational studies. Green shading indicates low risk, yellow shading indicates unclear risk, orange shading indicates high risk, and grey lines indicate that the question is not applicable to that study. Question 12 of the single-arm trial risk of bias tool assesses risk associated with interventions at the group level; however, this question was not applicable for any of the studies and therefore is not included in the figure. The assessment questions are listed in Table 2. Abbreviations: D, domain; Qu, question

sec.title: Efficacy/effectiveness outcomes
sec.p: The proportion of participants with viral suppression was reported for 15 populations across 12 studies (Fig. 3a and c; Additional File 1, Section S7). In Mujuru 2023, assessing DRV/cobicistat, 86% (6/7) of treatment-experienced children had undetectable viral load (< 50 copies/mL) at 48 weeks.

caption.p: Scatterplots summarizing the proportion of participants receiving ritonavir-boosted darunavir (a, c) or lopinavir (b, d) with undetectable viral load of < 50 copies/mL (a, b), or viral suppression of < 400 copies/mL or < 1000 copies/mL (c, d), categorized by age (colour of the symbol), treatment experience/viral suppression at the start of treatment (shape and fill of the symbol), and size of the study (size of the symbol). Data from an individual study may be presented at more than one time point (connected by lines); however, at a given time point, data from a group of participants are presented only once (overall data were prioritized over subgroup data). For clinical trials, the time point corresponds to the time between the start of treatment and the last follow-up visit; for observational studies, the time point refers to the median follow-up duration across participants. † One study labelled ‘treatment-experienced’ (EPPICC) included a small proportion of treatment-naïve participants (7% of the total study population). Note: In Panel b, the results from Gondrie 2017 were truncated at 208 weeks; beyond this time, data were available for < 65% of participants, and the proportion with undetectable VL remained within the 80–100% range (2/2 suppressed at 572 weeks): see Additional File 1, Table S5

sec.p: Across the 11 DRV/r studies, DIONE was the only study reporting disaggregated viral load data in treatment-naïve participants; 83% (10/12) had undetectable viral load at 48 weeks [27]. A high proportion of treatment-experienced participants who were virally suppressed at baseline had undetectable viral load at 48–52 weeks (two studies: 95% (143/150), 100% (12/12)) (Fig. 3a) [26, 31, 33]. Among those who were not suppressed at baseline, results were mixed, ranging from 29% (4/14) to 81% (17/21) (N = 205, seven populations, five studies) [28, 29, 34, 35, 37]. The four studies where < 70% had undetectable viral load included participants who were heavily treatment-experienced or had multidrug-resistant virus [29, 34, 35, 37]. At higher suppression thresholds, 59% (47/80) to 86% (198/229) of participants not suppressed at baseline (N = 679, seven populations, four studies) achieved viral loads of < 400 or < 1000 copies/mL, indicating clinically important reductions in viral load even if they did not reach < 50 copies/mL (Fig. 3c) [23, 29, 34–36].

Changes in CD4 values were reported in the DRV/cobicistat study and six DRV/r studies (Additional File 1, Section S7) [23, 26–29, 31, 37]. Among six treatment-experienced children receiving DRV/cobicistat, median CD4% increased by 2.5% (IQR 1.2, 3.4%) [31]. Among studies assessing DRV/r in populations tending to have suboptimal baseline CD4 levels (treatment-naïve populations (n = 1) or treatment-experienced populations that were not suppressed when DRV/r was started (n = 4)), the trend was an increase in CD4 count, ranging from + 102 to + 221 cells/µL at 48 weeks (Additional File 1, Section S7). In one study assessing adolescents who were virally suppressed when DRV/r was started, mean CD4 count decreased (-43.8 cells/µL) after 48 weeks from a median of 775 cells/µL at baseline [26, 31].

sec.title: Safety outcomes
sec.p: The number and proportion of participants experiencing safety outcomes are reported in Fig. 4, and details of events (where available) are shown in Table 4.

caption.p: Proportion (colour) and number (number in cells) of participants on darunavir or lopinavir with reported outcomes; ordered by study type (randomized controlled trials (RCTs), single-arm trials (SATs), observational studies) and follow-up (FU) time. For clinical trials other than ODYSSEY, the follow-up time corresponded to the time between treatment initiation and the last follow-up visit at which the outcome of interest was recorded; for observational studies and ODYSSEY, the follow-up time corresponded to the median duration of follow-up. † In CHAPAS-4, SMILE and KONCERT, adverse events leading to ART modification were reported. In KONCERT, the two events in the QD arm consisted of nausea and vomiting and led to a switch from QD to BID, and the event in the BID arm was neutropenia, which led to a switch from zidovudine to abacavir. ‡ In DELPHI, only clinical grade 3/4 events were reported. § Tuberculosis-coinfected participants. ¶ Results were for PI-based ART, n = 177/203 on LPV/r. Abbreviations: Population and trmt exp. (treatment experience) groups: I, infants; C, children; A, adolescents; E, treatment-experienced; N, treatment-naïve; mix, mixed or unspecified previous treatment experience. AE, adverse event; BID, twice daily; discont., discontinuation; DRV, darunavir; DTG, dolutegravir; FU, follow-up; INSTI, integrase strand-transfer inhibitor; LPV/r, ritonavir-boosted lopinavir; observ., observational; QD, once-daily; RCT, randomized controlled trial (including cluster-randomized trial); SAT, single-arm trial; SoC, standard-of-care

Details of grade 3/4 adverse events, drug-related grade 3/4 and serious adverse events, and ART-modifying events and events leading to treatment discontinuation (any grade) reported across studies of darunavir in children and adolescents

tr.th: Type of event and study name
Cause of death/details of adverse event†

td.p: Psychiatric plus metabolism and nutrition disorders

table-wrap-foot.p: † For each event reported, n = 1 unless otherwise specified. ‡ Only the most common events were reported. § ART-modifying events were reported in SMILE, whereas events leading to treatment discontinuation were reported in all other studies

sec.p: Among the nine treatment-experienced children who received DRV/cobicistat, there were no deaths, drug-related grade 3/4 adverse drug reactions or adverse events leading to discontinuation; one grade 3/4 adverse event was reported (Fig. 4; Table 4) [31].

Mortality in children and/or adolescents on DRV/r was reported in ten studies (12 sub-populations including the three separate DIANA cohorts) (N = 1824). Seven studies reported no deaths [23, 26–28, 30, 37, 38], whereas in the remaining three observational studies, 1% (5/431) to 3.5% (29/820) of participants died during follow-up, none due to treatment [32, 34, 36].

Grade 3/4 adverse events were reported in seven studies (nine sub-populations) and occurred in 0–30% of participants on DRV/r (N = 515) [23, 26–30, 38]. In addition, HIV-NAT 113 reported 14 grade 3/4 adverse events, but the number of participants who experienced these events was unclear (not shown in Fig. 4) [34]. There were no statistically significant differences in the proportion of grade 3/4 adverse events between the DRV/r and LPV/r study arms in CHAPAS-4 (9% and 11%, respectively, p = 0.31) or between the integrase strand transfer inhibitor (INSTI) + DRV/r (8%, 13/158) and standard-of-care (12%, 19/160) arms in SMILE (p = 0.28) [23, 26, 39].

Six studies (eight sub-populations) reported grade 3/4 adverse drug reactions (N = 179) [27–30, 34, 38], and only one reported events, occurring in 5.6% of children and adolescents in HIV-NAT 113 [34]. In addition, drug-related serious adverse events (grade not provided) were reported in 1.3% of adolescents on DRV for a median of 77.1 weeks in EPPICC (3/224 participants with clinical data) [32].

Data on discontinuations for adverse events were reported in eight studies (10 sub-populations) (N = 584), with proportions ranging from 0% to 10%, whereas CHAPAS-4 and SMILE reported ART-modifying events (N = 390) [23, 26–30, 32, 33, 37, 38]. A similar proportion of participants experienced ART-modifying events across study arms in SMILE (INSTI + DRV/r, 4/158; standard-of-care, 4/160; p = 0.99) and CHAPAS-4 (p > 0.05 comparing DRV/r, LPV/r, dolutegravir, and ritonavir-boosted atazanavir) [23, 24, 26].

sec.title: Lipid outcomes
sec.p: Six studies reported lipid outcomes in children and adolescents on DRV/r (Fig. 5, Additional File 1 (Section S8)) [23, 26, 28, 29, 34, 37]. There were few statistically significant changes in lipid outcomes between baseline and 48-week follow-up (96 weeks for CHAPAS-4). Two studies reported significant decreases in mean/median triglyceride levels, although for HIV-NAT 113, the difference was significant only among 10 participants with hyperlipidaemia at baseline [29, 34]. DELPHI reported significant increases in mean total cholesterol, low-density lipoprotein (LDL), and high-density lipoprotein (HDL) among 80 children and adolescents, whereas SMILE reported a significant decrease in HDL in 158 adolescents on an INSTI + DRV/r, with no other significant lipid changes at 48 weeks [26, 29, 39].

caption.p: Summary of lipid outcomes at the longest follow-up time in each study for a) darunavir-based regimens and b) lopinavir-based regimens. The results are stratified by whether the study assessed changes over time or differences between treatments. Studies are ordered by study design and alphabetically. Thick arrows indicate significant differences; thin arrows indicate the direction of effect when statistical significance was not reported; arrows pointing left-right indicate no significant difference. † N for lipid analysis not reported, but overall, 232 were on DRV/r, 227 on LPV/r, 229 on DTG, and 231 on ATV/r. ‡ n = 31 for total cholesterol. § Thresholds for hyperlipidaemia were TRIG ≥ 150 mg/dL, CHOL ≥ 200 mg/dL, LDL ≥ 130 mg/dL, and HDL < 40 mg/dL. Abbreviations: A, adolescents; ATV/r, ritonavir-boosted atazanavir; BID, twice daily; C, children; C + A, children and adolescents; CHOL, cholesterol; CS, cross-sectional study; DRV/r, ritonavir-boosted darunavir; DTG, dolutegravir; E, treatment-experienced; EFV, efavirenz; FU, follow-up; HDL, high-density lipoprotein; INSTI, integrase strand transfer inhibitor; LDL, low-density lipoprotein; LPV/r, ritonavir-boosted lopinavir; NNRTI, nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor; NR, not reported; QD, once daily; RCT, randomized controlled trial; SAT, single-arm trial; SOC, standard-of-care; TRIG, triglyceride; Trmt exp, treatment experience; vs., versus

sec.p: Two RCTs compared lipid outcomes between DRV/r and other treatments. CHAPAS-4 reported a significantly lower mean change in total cholesterol in children and adolescents on DRV/r compared with LPV/r (p < 0.001), and SMILE reported a lower mean change in HDL from baseline to 48 weeks in adolescents on INSTI + DRV/r than in those on standard-of-care (p = 0.003; Fig. 5a) [23, 26].

sec.title: Growth and development
sec.p: Six studies in children and adolescents on DRV/r reported changes in weight-for-age z scores (WAZ) from baseline, three of which also reported changes in height-for-age z scores (HAZ) (Fig. 6) [23, 26, 28, 29, 37, 38]. There were no significant changes in HAZ after 48 weeks on DRV/r-containing regimens [28, 29, 37]. WAZ at baseline ranged from a mean/median of -1.66 to -0.4, where reported (Fig. 6); two of four nonrandomized studies reported a statistically significant increase in WAZ during follow-up (change of + 0.20 in one study and from median -1.18 to -0.68 in the other) [29, 38]. CHAPAS-4 reported a statistically significant increase in WAZ of + 0.22 from baseline to 96 weeks (Fig. 6), which was similar in the DRV/r and ritonavir-boosted atazanavir arms [23]. In SMILE, WAZ in the INSTI + DRV/r arm was significantly greater than that in the standard-of-care arm over 48 weeks (adjusted difference in mean change in WAZ: 0.21 (95% CI 0.1, 0.3)) [26, 39].

caption.p: Summary of changes in height- and weight-for-age z scores for darunavir- and lopinavir-containing regimens at the latest available follow-up time. Treatment effects are summarized by arrows: thick arrows indicate significant differences; thin arrows indicate the direction of effect when statistical significance was not reported; arrows pointing left-right indicate no significant difference. † For clinical trials, the time point corresponds to the time between the start of treatment and the last follow-up visit; for observational studies, the time point refers to the median follow-up duration across participants. ‡ Data are reported in a figure; effect estimates were provided via correspondence with the CHAPAS-4 trial statistician. § Follow-up not specified, but 61% of the whole cohort started ART at < 6 months of age, and the median age at the time of analysis was 6.9 years. ¶ Adjusted for growth retardation, sex, age at diagnosis, opportunistic infection, and ART protocol. Abbreviations: A, adolescents (aged 12–19 years); aOR, adjusted odds ratio; ART, antiretroviral therapy; C, children (aged 0 to < 12 years); CI, confidence interval; CS, cross-sectional; C + A, children and adolescents; E, treatment-experienced; EFV, efavirenz; FU, follow-up; HAZ, height-for-age z score; I + C, infants and children; IQR, interquartile range; INSTI, integrase strand transfer inhibitor; L-S, liquid-to-solid lopinavir formulation switch (oral solution to pellets); LPV/r, ritonavir-boosted lopinavir; N, treatment-naïve; NR, not reported; NS, not statistically significant; RCT, randomized controlled trial; SAT, single-arm trial; SD, standard deviation; SOC, standard-of-care; Trmt exp, treatment experience; U, unclear treatment experience; vs., versus; WAZ, weight-for-age z score

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p: Across the 29 studies assessing LPV (all LPV/r) (Table 3), 3,605 infants, children and adolescents were included across three RCTs [23, 40, 41, 43, 44], one cluster RCT [42], four single-arm trials [21, 22, 45–49], 12 cohort studies [50–66] and nine cross-sectional studies [20, 67–74]. In the ODYSSEY RCT, LPV/r was not one of the randomized arms but rather an option within the standard-of-care arm; we included data from the LPV/r subgroup where available [43]. Eight studies were undertaken across multiple countries, and the remainder reported data from one country. Since we excluded studies assessing LPV/r syrup formulations, we identified few studies that included infants (n = 4); three of these studies also included children [42, 48, 58, 60]. Five studies reported on children, whereas 20 studies included both children and adolescents (Table 3). Four studies reported only treatment-naïve participants (n = 630) [42, 50, 57, 58], and one additional study reported disaggregated data on a subgroup that was treatment naïve. Six studies specified that all or most of the study population received tablet or capsule formulations of LPV/r, and four reported on LPV/r pellet or granule formulations [42, 48, 58, 60]. For the remaining studies, we assumed that most participants received solid formulations of LPV/r on the basis of the mean/median age of the study population.

sec.title: Risk of bias
sec.p: Results from the risk of bias assessment are presented in Fig. 2 and Additional File 1 (Section S5). Risk of bias was low for two RCTs (CHAPAS-4, ODYSSEY) and unclear for KONCERT and Life. One single-arm trial (LIVING) was classified as low, and the other three were classified as having an unclear risk of bias. Across the 21 observational studies, eight were classified as low risk of bias, and 12 were classified as high risk of bias.

sec.title: Efficacy/effectiveness outcomes
sec.p: The proportion of participants with viral suppression in each study is presented in Fig. 3b and d and Additional File 1 (Section S7) (three studies reported only < 50 copies/mL, five reported only < 400 or < 1000 copies/mL, and two reported < 50 and < 1000 copies/mL). At 48–52 weeks, no study reported the proportion of patients with a viral load < 50 copies/mL for treatment-naïve populations; among treatment-experienced populations, those with a viral load < 50 copies/mL ranged from 53% (448/852) to 100% (12/12) (4 studies, Fig. 3a) [45, 49, 52, 66]. For higher thresholds (< 400 and < 1000 copies/mL), the lowest suppression rates were reported in a study of treatment-naïve infants: at 52 weeks, 50% and 46% were suppressed (n not reported) among those diagnosed early and using standard practice, respectively [42]. Among treatment-experienced populations, viral suppression < 400 or < 1000 copies/mL was reported in > 60% of participants (5 studies, 10 data points), including during the 96-week follow-up in CHAPAS-4 (≥ 80% at timepoints > 24 weeks, n = 223) (Fig. 3d) [23, 49, 60, 61, 63].

Six studies reported changes in CD4 count or percentage from baseline (Additional File 1, Table S6) [23, 40, 41, 45, 57, 61, 66]. Across studies assessing populations that tended to have suboptimal baseline CD4 levels (treatment-naïve (n = 1) or treatment-experienced and not suppressed at LPV/r initiation (n = 3)), the change in CD4 count at 48–52 weeks ranged from + 96 cells/µL to + 256 cells/µL (Additional File 1, Section S7) [23, 66]. Across studies with populations that were virally suppressed at LPV/r initiation (two studies, three arms), changes at 48 weeks were small and not significant [40, 45].

sec.title: Safety outcomes
sec.p: Mortality in children and/or adolescents on LPV/r was reported in 13 studies and ranged from 0% to 6.7% in 12 studies (N = 2290, Fig. 4); few details on cause of death were provided [21–23, 40–49, 51–54, 56–66]. The thirteenth study, the Life cluster-RCT, reported a greater proportion of deaths (20–22%) among very young, newly diagnosed infants in low-income settings receiving LPV/r granules [42]. In the two studies reporting mortality data in infants and/or young children receiving LPV/r pellets, deaths occurred in < 2% of participants, and none were considered treatment-related [48, 60] (Fig. 4).

Grade 3/4 adverse events following LPV/r treatment were reported in four studies (N = 1405) and occurred in 8%-60% of participants [23, 40, 49, 61]. The highest rate of grade 3/4 adverse events occurred in a small (N = 15) pharmacokinetic study of tuberculosis-coinfected participants failing nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI)-based ART and was mostly due to haemoglobin abnormalities or neutropenia [61]. In the two RCTs, there were no differences in the proportion of participants experiencing grade 3/4 adverse events between arms (CHAPAS-4: as reported above, p = 0.31; KONCERT: 8% in the twice-daily LPV/r arm and 13% in the once-daily arm, p = 0.3; Fig. 4) [23, 40]. Drug-related grade 3/4 adverse events were reported in five studies (N = 1107) [22, 45–47, 49, 61], with two reporting events (hypertriglyceridaemia, n = 2; gastrointestinal events, n = 5) [22, 49]. Discontinuations for adverse events of any grade were reported in six studies and occurred in 0–9% of participants (N = 400; Fig. 4), but few details were provided [22, 40, 46, 50, 52, 60, 61]. ART-modifying events were reported in 3.1% of participants on LPV/r in CHAPAS-4 (7/227), with no difference across the four treatment arms (p > 0.5), and in 1–2% of participants in the KONCERT trial (Fig. 4) [23, 24, 40].

sec.title: Lipid outcomes
sec.p: Eight studies reported the associations between LPV/r and lipid outcomes over time, six of which reported outcomes at ≥ 96 weeks (Fig. 5, Additional File 1 (Section S8)) [20–23, 40, 45–47, 52, 69]. Four studies reported significant increases in total cholesterol from baseline [20, 22, 23, 69]. Other statistically significant changes over time in participants on LPV/r were an increase in LDL at 96 weeks in CHAPAS-4 [23], an increase in HDL at 96 weeks in HIV-NAT 017 [22], and an increase in triglyceride levels at 296 weeks in Dejkhamron 2014 [69].

Six studies reported eight comparisons of lipid levels with LPV/r versus other regimens [20, 23, 40, 51, 67, 71]. Compared with other regimens, LPV/r was associated with significantly increased triglyceride levels (3 of 4 comparisons), total cholesterol (6 of 8 comparisons), and LDL cholesterol (4 of 8 comparisons) (Fig. 5). The CHAPAS-4 RCT reported a significantly greater mean change in total cholesterol in children and adolescents on LPV/r for 96 weeks than in those on DRV/r, ritonavir-boosted atazanavir, or dolutegravir (Fig. 5) [23].

sec.title: Growth and development
sec.p: Five studies reported HAZ-related outcomes in populations treated with LPV/r (Fig. 6) [21, 22, 46, 47, 67, 69, 70]. Among three studies reporting median change from baseline, two reported a significant increase, whereas in a third study, statistical significance was not reported (baseline HAZ ranged from -2.1 to -0.97) [21, 22, 46, 47, 69].

Change in WAZ from baseline were reported in five studies across seven populations (Fig. 6) [21–23, 46–48, 69]. Baseline and follow-up summary statistics were reported for six populations; mean/median WAZ at baseline ranged from -1.8 to -0.5. At last follow-up, there was a statistically significant increase in median WAZ in two studies (Fig. 6) [21, 22, 46, 47]. In CHAPAS-4, there was no change in WAZ from baseline to 96 weeks in children and adolescents on LPV/r; in contrast, the trial reported significant increases in WAZ for ritonavir-boosted atazanavir, DRV/r, and dolutegravir [23].

Three studies reported neuropsychological outcomes [53, 72, 73]. Two studies reported that children on LPV/r had significantly higher neuropsychological scores than those on NNRTI regimens (on cognitive ability, attention-impulsivity, and motor domain tests, but not on executive function, n = 164 on LPV/r; and on motor domains but not manual coordination, strength/agility, or neurocognitive function, n = 34 on LPV/r) [53, 72]. A third study revealed no differences in overall intelligence quotient, verbal comprehension, perceptual reasoning, working memory or processing speed between LPV/r-based regimens (n = 14) and normative samples [73].

sec.title: Additional outcomes
sec.p: For both DRV and LPV/r, prespecified outcomes on which limited data were identified are described in Additional File 1 (Section S6). Exploratory outcomes not prespecified in our protocol but identified during screening (e.g., persistent renal dysfunction and lipodystrophy) are reported in Additional File 1, along with associated study characteristics and risk of bias assessment (Section S9, ‘Exploratory analyses’).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The findings from this systematic review suggest that DRV/r is effective in treatment-experienced children and adolescents. Data were too limited to draw conclusions for treatment-naïve participants (one study reported disaggregated data, n = 12) [27] or participants receiving DRV/cobicistat (one study, n = 9) [31]. No studies included infants or young children, as expected, given that DRV is not licenced for use in children < 3 years old [2]. Viral suppression with DRV/r was high in RCTs (> 85%), including long-term follow-up of CHAPAS-4 up to 96 weeks in participants who had experienced treatment failure on NNRTI-based first-line treatment [23] (similar results were reported up to 168 weeks; reported after our search dates [24]). Poorer viral suppression was reported in studies of treatment-experienced participants who were not suppressed at the start of DRV/r; nevertheless, clinically significant reductions in viral load (to < 400 copies/mL or < 1000 copies/mL) were observed in 59–100% of participants, many of whom had complex treatment histories. Future studies should assess the long-term effectiveness of DRV/r in ‘real-world’ settings and when used after dolutegravir-based first-line regimens [2].

The safety profile of DRV/r was favourable, with few DRV-related grade 3/4 adverse events, few discontinuations due to adverse events, and no deaths attributed to DRV/r. Grade 3/4 adverse events were reported in 0–30% of participants, with the highest proportions reported in small single-arm trials in heavily treatment-experienced participants. In CHAPAS-4 and SMILE, < 10% of participants on DRV/r experienced grade 3/4 adverse events, and there were no differences between DRV/r and other study arms in the proportion experiencing grade 3/4 adverse events or ART-modifying events [23, 26]. The types of grade 3/4 adverse events and those leading to discontinuation across the studies varied and included respiratory, gastrointestinal, psychiatric and other events.

There were few significant changes in lipids across the DRV/r studies, with levels tending to remain within normal ranges. It is unclear whether these small changes are clinically significant, particularly given that most studies had only 48 weeks of follow-up [26, 28, 29]. Reassuringly, in the CHAPAS-4 trial, there were no significant changes from baseline to 96 weeks in total or LDL cholesterol, and the change in total cholesterol was significantly lower in participants on DRV/r than in those on LPV/r. Long-term observational studies monitoring lipid levels in children and adolescents on DRV-based regimens are needed to corroborate these findings. There was no significant change in HAZ (in all three studies) and limited evidence of an increase in WAZ (in three of six studies).

Safety and effectiveness results for LPV/r solid formulations in children and adolescents were mixed, with viral suppression (< 400 or < 1000 copies/mL) at 48–52 weeks reported in 46–72% of treatment-naïve participants and 64–84% of treatment-experienced participants. The CHAPAS-4 RCT demonstrated that dolutegravir was superior to LPV/r and ritonavir-boosted atazanavir arms combined in terms of suppression < 400 copies/mL, and DRV/r was noninferior, with a trend toward superiority [23]. Secondary analyses revealed significantly poorer suppression (< 400 copies/mL) with LPV/r than with dolutegravir or DRV/r [23]. The poorest virological outcomes were reported in a cluster-randomized trial in infants (aged 0–8 weeks) and a study in young children (median age 1.8 years), both of which were in sub-Saharan Africa [42, 60]. The cluster-RCT also reported the poorest mortality outcomes (20–22%), but this may reflect high mortality rates in newly diagnosed infants in sub-Saharan Africa rather than treatment with LPV/r. Across other studies, mortality ranged from 0% to 7%.

Although there are limited data on grade 3/4 adverse events following LPV/r treatment, there were no significant differences between once- and twice-daily LPV/r dosing in the KONCERT trial or between LPV/r and DRV/r or dolutegravir in CHAPAS-4 [23, 40, 41]. Drug-related grade 3/4 adverse events were uncommon, whereas discontinuations for adverse events occurred in less than 10% of infants, children and adolescents across seven studies.

There were some indications that total cholesterol may increase over time in children and adolescents on LPV/r (significant increase in 3/8 studies). Additionally, five of six studies reported that children and adolescents on LPV/r had significantly higher levels of triglycerides and/or total cholesterol than did those on other drugs (including dolutegravir, NNRTIs, and other PIs). In adults, elevated cholesterol has been associated with increased all-cause mortality [75] and increased medical costs [76]. Maintaining lipid levels within normal ranges is important for the long-term health and well-being of people who acquire HIV in early life, as they will require lifelong treatment. Although the clinical significance of these observed lipid increases is unclear, monitoring lipid outcomes in both children and adults is important for obtaining a full understanding of ART safety profiles.

In terms of growth, two of the five studies reported a significant increase in WAZ over time among participants on LPV/r. However, CHAPAS-4 revealed no change in mean WAZ between baseline and 96 weeks for children and adolescents on LPV/r (those who had low z scores at baseline) but reported significant (age appropriate) increases in WAZ for dolutegravir, ritonavir-boosted atazanavir, and DRV/r, suggesting that LPV/r may be associated with comparatively poorer weight gain [23].

To our knowledge, this is the first systematic review assessing the effectiveness and safety of DRV/r, DRV/cobicistat and LPV/r solid formulations in infants, children and adolescents living with HIV. The evidence is broadly reassuring but has several limitations. There are limited long-term effectiveness data for DRV/r, as the longest follow-up was 96 weeks, and very limited data exist for treatment-naïve populations and participants receiving DRV/cobicistat. Further data are also needed on outcomes in infants and young children on LPV/r in routine care and in children and adolescents on DRV/r and LPV/r after dolutegravir failure. Many studies (27/41) were observational and therefore prone to greater risk of bias; indeed, 14/27 were rated as having a high risk of bias. In addition, sample sizes were often small (25/41 studies included < 100 participants), and some studies may therefore have lacked the power to assess treatment effects. Across the two drugs, there were limited data on adverse events from observational studies, highlighting the need for standardized outcome definitions to optimize long-term safety monitoring. We were unable to evaluate the importance of adherence for effectiveness outcomes in this review. Owing to issues with reporting LPV/r regimens and formulations, we amended the inclusion criteria for LPV/r studies after our protocol was finalized; however, we believe that the criteria and cut-offs that we selected ensured that the included studies were as closely aligned to our research question as possible.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: The findings from this systematic review suggest that DRV/r is safe and effective in children and adolescents, although further large-scale studies of DRV/r in these populations in real-world clinical settings are needed, particularly where DRV/r follows failure of dolutegravir-based regimens. Comparative data from CHAPAS-4 indicate that DRV/r-based regimens are noninferior (with a trend toward superiority) to combined data from ritonavir-boosted atazanavir and LPV/r-based regimens in terms of viral suppression. The development of a paediatric fixed-dose combination formulation of DRV/r (120 mg DRV plus 20 mg ritonavir) will be a valuable addition to the list of available, safe and effective treatments for children and adolescents living with HIV if the results of the UNIVERSAL-2 trial are favourable [5]. The results from the ongoing GS-US-216-0128 study (preliminary results presented in Mujuru 2023) will provide important insights into the role of DRV/cobicistat in young populations [6, 31]. LPV/r-based regimens are widely used as the preferred second-line treatment for infants and children failing dolutegravir-based therapy [2] and remain a valuable option considering the limited alternative formulations for this age group. However, we found mixed results in terms of the effectiveness and safety of LPV/r solid formulations, and there is increasing evidence of poorer lipid and growth outcomes than with other PI-based regimens, which requires further investigation. Treatment recommendations on preferred regimens for treatment-experienced patients require careful consideration of these findings and the relative merits of these two PIs to maximize treatment effectiveness and minimize the long-term impact on lipid and growth outcomes.

notes.title: Author contributions
